sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 5 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, UP, UP, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 26, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Pair back at (45, 34). The white cross has REAPPEARED in the left corridor, meaning it respawns when the pair returns to start. Simply collecting and returning doesn't complete the level. Need to explore the right side of the maze which hasn't been visited yet.
- Plan: Navigate to the right side of the maze to explore unvisited areas.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 25 actions; a strong score needs about 22 or fewer. You are 1.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The pair is now at (30, 44) - on the right side of the maze. The white cross is still visible at (31, 21) in the left corridor. No new items appear on the right side.
I've now explored:
1. Top room - inaccessible
2. Left vertical corridor - has the white cross
3. Right vertical corridor - no special items
4. Starting position - cross respawns here
The white cross is the only collectible item. Let me reconsider the goal:
- Maybe the goal is to collect the cross and take it to the top room (but the pair can't enter)
- Maybe the goal is to collect the cross and stay at a specific location
- Maybe the goal involves matching the pair's position to the blue L-shape pattern
Wait, let me look at the blue L-shapes:
1. Top room: blue L-shape at (11-13, 35-37)
2. Bottom-left: blue L-shape at (55-60, 3-8)
These are in black-framed areas. Maybe the goal is to position the pair so that it "completes" a pattern with one of these L-shapes?
Or maybe I need to think about this differently. The pair consists of an orange 2x4 block and a blue 3x4 block. The white cross is a 3-pixel cross shape.
Let me try a different approach: collect the cross and then try to go to the top room again. Maybe after collecting the cross, the pair can enter the top room?
From current position (30, 44):
- DOWN, DOWN, DOWN → (45, 44)
- LEFT, LEFT → (45, 34)
- UP, UP, UP, UP, UP → (20, 34)
- UP → try to enter top room
Actually, that's a lot of actions. Let me think more carefully.
Wait, I notice the white cross is at (31, 21). When I was at (30, 19) before, the pair covered rows 30-34, cols 19-23. The cross at (31, 21) would be within that area, which is why it disappeared.
Now the pair is at (30, 44), covering rows 30-34, cols 44-48. The cross is still visible at (31, 21).
Let me try collecting the cross again and then immediately trying to go to the top room. The route would be:
From (30, 44):
- LEFT, LEFT, LEFT → (30, 19) - collect the cross
- UP → (25, 19)
- RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT → (25, 34)
- UP, UP, UP, UP → (5, 34) - but wait, the narrow corridor starts at row 16, so (5, 34) might not be valid
Actually, let me reconsider. The narrow corridor is at rows 16-24, col 34. From (25, 34), UP goes to (20, 34), then (15, 34) - but row 15 is the boundary of the top room. The pair can't go further up from (20, 34) because it's blocked.
Hmm, maybe I need to approach the top room differently. Let me check if there's another way to enter it.
Actually, looking at the image, the top room has an opening at the bottom. The pair can move up to row 20 in the narrow corridor, but can't enter the top room. The top room is at rows 9-15.
Wait, let me re-examine. The narrow corridor at col 34 goes from row 16 to row 24. The top room is at rows 9-15, cols 33-39. There's a small connection between them.
Maybe the pair needs to be in a specific orientation or state to enter the top room. Or maybe the goal isn't to enter the top room at all.
Let me try a different hypothesis: maybe the goal is to collect the cross and then position the pair at a specific location that matches the blue L-shape pattern.
Actually, let me just try collecting the cross and then going to the top room area to see if anything different happens.
From (30, 44):
- LEFT × 3 → (30, 19) - collect cross
- UP → (25, 19)
- RIGHT × 3 → (25, 34)
- UP × 4 → (5, 34) - try to reach top
Wait, from (25, 34), UP goes to (20, 34), then (15, 34), then (10, 34), then (5, 34). But the narrow corridor only allows rows 16-24. So from (20, 34), UP was blocked before.
Let me try collecting the cross first and see if that changes anything about the top room accessibility.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Pair at (30, 44) on right side. White cross still visible at (31, 21) in left corridor. No new items on right side. The cross respawns when pair returns to start. Need to test if collecting the cross enables access to the top room.
Plan: Collect the cross (LEFT×3 to reach it), then try going to the top room to see if accessibility changes.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>

# Collect cross: (30,44) → (30,19)
result = action(['LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT'])
print(f"Collect cross: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}, done={last_action_result.get('done')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        print(f"Orange: boundary={n['boundary']}")
    if n['color'] == 'W':
        print(f"White: boundary={n['boundary']}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Now try UP to see if top room is accessible after collecting cross
result2 = action(['UP', 'UP', 'UP', 'UP', 'UP'])
print(f"After UPx5: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}, done={last_action_result.get('done')}")

seg2 = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg2:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        print(f"Orange: boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Collect cross: board_changed=True, done=False
Orange: boundary=[[30, 34], [30, 38], [31, 38], [31, 34]]
White: boundary=[[31, 21], [32, 22], [32, 21]], pixels=3
After UPx5: board_changed=True, done=False
Orange: boundary=[[40, 29], [40, 33], [41, 33], [41, 29]]
